findings.title: Unbounded signals list: state-condition rules (above/below/rising) fire on every bar, producing O(N) entries per visualized rule
findings.detail: eval_rule() for conditions 'above', 'below', 'rising', 'falling' returns True on virtually every bar when the condition is broadly true (e.g. 'price above EMA20 in an uptrend'). With 100K 1-min bars and 1 such visualized rule, the signals list accumulates ~70K–90K dicts before JSON serialization. Each dict is {time, price}, so at ~40 bytes each that is 3–4 MB of Python objects per rule before the JSON encoder runs. The JSON response itself can easily hit 5–10 MB for 4 such rules. The frontend must then parse and render all markers. This is a data-volume problem, not a CPU problem.
findings.suggested_fix: Limit signal markers to event-type conditions (crossover, turns_up, turns_down) where the signal count is naturally sparse. For state conditions (above, below, rising, falling), emit only the bar where the condition transitions from False to True (i.e., the entry edge), not every bar where it holds. This can be done with a one-line prev_fired tracker inside the per-rule loop, or by only collecting signals for conditions in a whitelist of event-type conditions and skipping state conditions for visualization altogether.

findings.title: Second full-bar pass for visualized rules duplicates work already done in main backtest loop
findings.detail: The main loop at line 148 calls eval_rules() (which calls eval_rule() for each rule) on every bar already. The new second pass at lines 438-451 re-evaluates the same rules over the same bars. For the common case (100K bars, 4 visualized rules), this is an extra 400K eval_rule() calls. Each call does a dict lookup (resolve_series), two pandas .iloc accesses, a string comparison chain, and possibly resolve_ref(). At CPython overhead, each call costs roughly 1–5 µs, putting the second pass at 0.4–2 seconds of pure Python on top of the already-completed backtest. This is not blocking (the backtest is synchronous) but doubles the per-request latency budget attributable to rule evaluation.
findings.suggested_fix: Collect signals during the existing main loop at line 148 instead of a second pass. Inside the loop, after eval_rule() is called for each rule, conditionally append to a per-rule accumulator if rule.visualize is True. This eliminates the second traversal entirely. The accumulators can be pre-allocated as {rule_index: []} dicts before the loop.

findings.title: turns_up/turns_down with threshold does a backwards linear scan per bar (O(N^2) worst case for visualized rules)
findings.detail: When condition is 'turns_up' or 'turns_down' with a non-null threshold, eval_rule() scans backward from i to find the true trough/peak (lines 410-415, 431-436). This scan continues until the series reverses direction, which in a long smooth trend can traverse hundreds of bars. When this rule is visualized and the second pass iterates all 100K bars, the total work is O(N * avg_scan_depth). In a 100K-bar smooth trend dataset, avg_scan_depth can be O(N), making this O(N^2) = 10^10 operations in the pathological case. This bug pre-existed B4 but the second visualization pass now exercises it on every bar rather than only on bars where the backtest logic reaches that rule.
findings.suggested_fix: Pre-compute a 'previous reversal index' array for the series before entering the bar loop, so each bar's trough/peak lookup is O(1). Alternatively, cache the last computed trough/peak and only rescan when the lookback window shifts. The simplest bounded fix is to cap the backward scan at a configurable max_lookback (e.g. 200 bars) matching typical technical analysis practice.

residual_risks.description: No server-side cap on the number of visualized rules. A user could mark all rules as visualize=True. With 10 rules on 100K bars, signals payload could exceed 50 MB for state conditions, potentially OOMing or timing out the response.

resolve_series() and resolve_ref() are called on every bar inside eval_rule() and perform dict key construction (string formatting, .get() lookup) per call. At 400K calls this is measurable but not blocking — it becomes more relevant if the second-pass finding is not addressed and bar counts grow.

testing_gaps.description: No test exercises a visualized rule with condition 'above' or 'below' over a large bar series to verify the signals list is bounded or trimmed before serialization.
No benchmark or latency regression test for the /api/backtest endpoint at 100K bars with visualized rules. Without a baseline, the second-pass overhead will not be caught by CI.
turns_up/turns_down with threshold and a large smooth trend segment is not covered by any test, so the O(N^2) scan depth risk is invisible in current test runs.
